content.text: Developer Docs and Runbooks in the Portal

Documentation in an IDP context is not a wiki — it is structured content attached to specific catalog entities and surfaced in context. An engineer looking at the invoice-assistant catalog entry should see its getting-started guide, its API reference, its runbook, and its architectural decision records — without leaving the IDP. Documentation that lives only in a wiki requires engineers to know to look for it; documentation linked from the catalog is discovered when it is needed. For Claude integrations specifically, the documentation must cover four types of content: getting-started guides, API reference for shared libraries, operational runbooks for incident response, and architectural decision records for prompt and model choices.

attrs.alt: Diagram: four documentation types in the IDP portal — Getting Started Guides, API Reference, Operational Runbooks, and Architecture Decision Records

content.text: Four Documentation Types
Type
Audience
When consulted
Owner
Review cadence
Getting Started Guide
Engineers new to Claude integrations
When starting a new integration or onboarding to an existing one
Platform / developer experience team
Each major SDK or scaffold version bump
API Reference
Engineers writing integration code
During development; when debugging client library behaviour
Platform team (auto-generated from code)
Each library release
Operational Runbook
On-call engineers during incidents
When an alert fires or a user-visible issue is detected
Feature team that owns the integration
Before production launch; after each incident
Architecture Decision Record (ADR)
Reviewers, future engineers, security reviewers
During code review; when a past decision is questioned
Feature team; prompt lead for model and safety choices
When a decision is made or reversed
Getting Started Guides
What the guide covers

The getting-started guide is the entry point for any engineer building a new Claude integration. It is linked from the IDP home page and from every app scaffold. A good getting-started guide covers the full paved road from blank state to a staged, monitored integration:
Install the app scaffold from the IDP catalog (one command; creates repo with CI, client library, sample evals).
Create your system prompt in the prompts/ registry (YAML template with capability list, safety baseline, token budget).
Wire the prompt template to the client library (template_id reference, not copy-paste).
Write 10 golden dataset cases covering your DO and DO NOT items.
Run the CI gate pipeline locally (npm run ci:gates) and fix any failures.
Open a PR; security reviewer is automatically added by CODEOWNERS.
After merge: provision a staging environment via the IDP portal.
Monitor the scorecard for the first two sprints; resolve any amber metrics.
Guide maintenance

Getting-started guides rot when the tools they describe change. Every major version bump in the client library or scaffold must trigger a review of the guide. The platform team owns this review; the guide should be checked against the actual scaffold output by following it verbatim once per quarter:

# Quarterly guide validation (run by platform team)
1. Create a new git repository
2. Follow the getting-started guide exactly as written
3. Note any step that fails, is unclear, or references a deprecated tool
4. Update the guide and open a PR before the end of the week
5. Merge and update the IDP guide link
API Reference for Client Libraries
Auto-generation from docstrings

API reference for the shared Claude client libraries is generated automatically from source code docstrings. Engineers should never need to maintain a separate API reference document — the reference is always current because it is derived from the code:

# src/claude_client/client.py
class ClaudeClient:
    """Shared Anthropic API client with built-in safety baseline injection,
    retry logic, and cost telemetry.

    Usage::

        client = ClaudeClient.from_env()
        response = await client.create_message(
            model="claude-sonnet-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=template.system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": message}],
        )

    All calls automatically:
    - Inject the current safety baseline into the system prompt
    - Log input_tokens, output_tokens, and stop_reason
    - Retry on RateLimitError (3× with exponential backoff)
    - Raise BudgetExceededError if the environment cost cap is reached
    """

    @classmethod
    def from_env(cls) -> "ClaudeClient":
        """Create a client from environment variables.

        Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY_SECRET_ARN from the environment and fetches
        the API key from AWS Secrets Manager. Reads CLAUDE_SERVICE_NAME to
        identify the rate-limit allocation to use.

        Raises:
            ConfigurationError: if required environment variables are missing
            SecretsAccessError: if the API key secret cannot be read
        """

    async def create_message(
        self,
        model: str,
        max_tokens: int,
        system: str,
        messages: list[dict],
        **kwargs,
    ) -> anthropic.types.Message:
        """Send a message to the Claude API.

        Args:
            model: Claude model ID (must be on the approved model list).
            max_tokens: Maximum output tokens. Required — there is no default.
            system: System prompt text. The safety baseline is appended
                    automatically; do not include it manually.
            messages: List of message dicts in Anthropic Messages API format.
            **kwargs: Additional kwargs passed to anthropic.messages.create().

        Returns:
            anthropic.types.Message with usage, stop_reason, and content.

        Raises:
            BudgetExceededError: monthly cost cap for this environment is reached.
            RateLimitError: rate limit not resolved after 3 retries.
            UnapprovedModelError: model is not on the approved model list.
        """

The CI pipeline runs pdoc (or Sphinx) on every merge to main, generating HTML reference docs from these docstrings. The IDP links the generated reference directly from the library's catalog entry — engineers click 'API Reference' in the catalog and see current, accurate method signatures.
Operational Runbooks
What a runbook must contain

A runbook is a decision-tree document for on-call engineers. It is not a narrative explanation of how the integration works — it is a step-by-step guide for diagnosing and resolving specific failure scenarios. Every Claude integration must have a runbook before it ships to production. The runbook covers a fixed set of scenarios:
Scenario
Trigger
Resolution path
High error rate (> 3%)
PagerDuty alert: error_rate_alert

1. Check Grafana dashboard for error type breakdown. 2. If RateLimitError: check TPM usage; contact platform for quota increase. 3. If AuthError: check secret rotation status. 4. If overload_error: wait 60s and monitor.
P95 latency spike (> 90% of SLA)
PagerDuty alert: latency_sla_alert
1. Check context size in recent logs. 2. If context grew: verify context cap is applied. 3. If Anthropic upstream latency: check status.anthropic.com. 4. Escalate if > 120% of SLA for 10+ min.
Cost budget exceeded
Budget alert: budget_exceeded_error in logs

1. Check cost breakdown in Grafana. 2. If token count grew: check for context cap regression. 3. Contact EM to approve temporary cap increase or throttle traffic. 4. Do not restart pods — restart does not reduce cost.
Eval pass rate drop (discovered in weekly scorecard)
Scorecard: quality status Red

1. Run eval suite against current staging prompt. 2. Identify failing cases. 3. If model update caused regression: open prompt issue. 4. If new user input distribution: add cases to golden dataset. 5. Do not ship until pass rate recovers.
Rollback required
On-call decision or canary auto-abort

1. Run: kubectl set image deployment/invoice-assistant app=invoice-assistant:prior-version. 2. Verify error rate falls within 2 minutes. 3. Post in #incidents with root cause and rollback version. 4. Open post-mortem issue.
Runbook template

# Runbook: invoice-assistant

**Owner:** billing-team
**On-call rotation:** https://pagerduty.com/schedules/invoice-assistant
**Dashboard:** https://grafana.internal/claude/invoice-assistant
**Scorecard:** https://dashboards.internal/scorecard/invoice-assistant
**Last reviewed:** 2025-06-01

## Scenarios

### 1. High error rate (> 3%)

**Alert:** `invoice_assistant_error_rate > 0.03` for 5 consecutive minutes

**Diagnosis:**
1. Open dashboard → Error Rate panel → expand by `error_type` label.
2. If majority are `RateLimitError`: the integration is over its TPM quota.
   → Contact #platform with current TPM usage; request temporary increase.
3. If majority are `AuthenticationError`: the API key may have expired.
   → Check AWS Secrets Manager: `/claude/staging/invoice-assistant/anthropic-key`
   → Trigger rotation if status is `expired`.
4. If majority are `overload_error`: upstream Anthropic capacity issue.
   → Check https://status.anthropic.com
   → Monitor for 5 minutes; escalate to P2 if persisting.

**Resolution SLA:** < 30 minutes to mitigation

---

### 2. Budget exceeded

**Trigger:** `BudgetExceededError` appears in application logs

**Diagnosis:**
1. Open dashboard → Cost panel → check token usage breakdown.
2. Compare current cost/request to baseline (see `token_budget.yml`).
3. If cost/request has grown > 20%: likely context size regression.
   → Check `build_context()` logs for token count.
4. If cost/request is stable but volume increased: traffic growth.
   → Contact EM for budget cap increase or traffic throttling decision.

**Do NOT:** restart pods. Restart does not reduce token cost.
**Do NOT:** increase the cap without EM approval.
Runbook links from monitoring alerts

Runbooks only help if they are found during an incident. Every monitoring alert must include the runbook URL in its alert message so the on-call engineer does not spend time searching during a live incident:

# Grafana alert rule annotation
annotations:
  summary: "invoice-assistant error rate high ({{ $value | humanizePercentage }})"
  description: |
    Error rate has exceeded 3% for 5 consecutive minutes.
    Runbook: https://wiki.internal/runbooks/invoice-assistant#high-error-rate
    Dashboard: https://grafana.internal/claude/invoice-assistant
    Scorecard: https://dashboards.internal/scorecard/invoice-assistant
Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
Why ADRs matter for Claude integrations

Claude integrations involve a higher density of non-obvious decisions than typical API integrations: which model to use and why, why the safety baseline was written the way it was, why a particular capability was placed in the DO NOT list, why the context window size was set to a specific value. These decisions are made under time pressure and with information that may not be obvious to a reviewer six months later. An ADR records the decision, the context, and the alternatives considered, so future engineers can understand why the codebase looks the way it does without having to reconstruct the reasoning.
ADR template for Claude integrations

# ADR-007: Use claude-sonnet-5 instead of claude-haiku for invoice explanations

**Date:** 2025-05-12
**Status:** Accepted
**Deciders:** Alice (tech lead), Bob (PM), Carol (security reviewer)

## Context

The invoice-assistant integration initially used claude-haiku-4-5 for cost reasons.
In eval testing, haiku's explanation quality on complex multi-line invoices scored
74% on the golden dataset — below the 85% amber threshold. The PM confirmed that
invoice explanation accuracy directly affects customer trust and escalation rates.

## Decision

Switch from claude-haiku-4-5 to claude-sonnet-5 for all invoice queries.

## Consequences

**Positive:**
- Eval pass rate rose from 74% to 93% on the same golden dataset.
- Injection probe pass rate remained at 100%.

**Negative:**
- Cost per request increased by ~4× (haiku → sonnet pricing difference).
- The monthly cost cap was raised from $20 to $80 to accommodate this.

## Alternatives considered

1. **Prompt engineering to improve haiku performance:** Tested 3 alternative prompts;
   best result was 79%. Not sufficient to clear the amber threshold.
2. **Hybrid routing (haiku for simple, sonnet for complex invoices):**
   Rejected — the complexity classifier added latency and the complexity threshold
   was difficult to set without over-routing to sonnet anyway.

## Review

This decision should be reconsidered if:
- Anthropic releases a haiku version with improved reasoning.
- The monthly cost exceeds $120 for two consecutive months.
ADR storage and discovery in the IDP

ADRs are stored as Markdown files in the repository under docs/adr/. The IDP catalog entry for each integration links to its ADR directory. The Backstage TechDocs plugin renders the Markdown files directly in the portal so engineers do not need to navigate to GitHub to read them. New ADRs are required for:
Any change to the model_id in a template (model selection decisions must be documented).
Any change to the safety_baseline_version (explains why the upgrade or downgrade was made).
Any capability added to or removed from the DO / DO NOT list (explains the business and safety rationale).
Any change to context architecture (e.g., switching from full-history to sliding-window context).
Documentation Quality Gates
Documentation completeness is enforced as a release gate. The pre-deploy check script verifies that the required documentation exists and is not placeholder content before promotion to production:
Summary

Developer docs and runbooks in the IDP are organised into four types — getting-started guides, API reference, operational runbooks, and ADRs — each serving a different audience and consulted at a different time. Getting-started guides onboard engineers; API reference is consulted during development; runbooks are used during incidents; ADRs explain why. Documentation completeness is a release gate: the pre-deploy check blocks production promotion if the runbook, ADR directory, or getting-started guide is missing or incomplete.
Getting-started guides must be validated against the actual scaffold each quarter — they rot faster than most code.
API reference is auto-generated from docstrings; engineers update the source, not the docs.
Runbooks are discovery-optimised: every alert links directly to the relevant runbook section.
ADRs are required for model selection, safety baseline changes, and capability list changes — they document decisions that would otherwise be invisible to future reviewers.
Documentation completeness is a pre-deploy check, not a courtesy: missing runbooks block production promotion.
